title: Open Research & Reproducibility
text: Before we start

title: Open Research & Reproducibility
text:
About these workshop materials
These materials support the Open Research & Reproducibility workshop. They were developed for use at UCL, but much of the content is relevant to researchers, students, analysts and research support staff working in other settings.

title: Open Research & Reproducibility
text:
How to use these materials
This resource is designed to accompany the workshop, but it can also be used afterwards as a reference. You do not need to read it all in one go. Each chapter focuses on one part of the research workflow and includes practical points that you can return to when planning, analysing, documenting or sharing your work.
The chapters are written as support material rather than as slides. This means this version contains more explanation than the live presentation, so that it remains useful after the workshop.

title: Open Research & Reproducibility
text:
What the workshop covers
The workshop is structured around a practical research workflow:

Why open research and reproducibility matter
What reproducibility means in practice, and why openness is not the same as making everything public.
FAIR basics
How to make outputs findable, accessible, interoperable and reusable, including when data cannot be openly shared.
Project organisation and continuity
Folder structures, file naming, raw and derived data, lightweight versioning and project-level orientation.
Data Management Plans
DMPs as living documents that record decisions about data, roles, storage, access, sharing and restrictions.
Organising and tidying data
Data-file structure, spreadsheet risks, tidy data, missingness, exclusions and data dictionaries.
Documentation and protocols
READMEs, metadata, decision logs, changelogs, SOPs, ELNs, protocols and reporting guidance.
Coding for reproducible research
Readable code, checks, testing, versioning, light automation, sustainable computing and R Markdown/Quarto examples.
Publishing, PIDs and preparing for reuse
Repositories, persistent identifiers, preprints, licences, availability statements and responsible reuse.
Wrap-up and next steps
A practical action plan for applying the workshop to your own work.

title: Chapter 1 — Why Open Research and Reproducibility?
text: Chapter 1

title: Chapter 1 — Why Open Research and Reproducibility?
text:
Why Open Research and Reproducibility?
Open research is not about making everything public. It is about making research easier to understand, check, trust and reuse, within appropriate ethical, legal and practical boundaries.

title: Chapter 1 — Why Open Research and Reproducibility?
Chapter 1 — Why Open Research and Reproducibility?
text:
Why openness now?
Open research is no longer a niche movement or an optional add-on. Across research, concerns about irreproducible findings, undocumented analytical decisions and inaccessible outputs have led funders, journals and institutions to raise expectations around transparency and robustness1–3.
For most researchers, the issue is not ideology. It is practical risk.
Research projects often involve multiple datasets, changing teams, evolving analyses, sensitive information and informal decisions made under time pressure. Without clear organisation and documentation, it becomes difficult to explain what was done, why it was done and whether the same results could be produced again.
Open and reproducible practice helps reduce:

avoidable rework;
loss of project memory;
uncertainty about which file, script or output is current;
errors introduced through manual or undocumented steps;
barriers to collaboration, handover, publication and reuse.







A practical way to think about reproducibility



Could someone else understand what you did well enough to check it?
Could you understand it yourself if you came back to the project in six months?
If the answer is “probably not”, the workflow may need better organisation, clearer documentation or more explicit version control.

title: Chapter 1 — Why Open Research and Reproducibility?
text:
Reproducibility as a visible route
Reproducibility is sometimes framed narrowly as whether someone can re-run an analysis and get exactly the same result. That matters, but it is not the whole story.
A more useful starting point is whether someone with relevant expertise can follow the route taken through the research process: what was done, why it was done, what evidence was generated, what judgements were made, and how these led to the findings.



Longleat Maze. Photo: Niki Odolphie / Wikimedia Commons, CC BY 2.0.








UKRN definition of reproducibility



The UK Reproducibility Network describes reproducibility as research being sufficiently transparent that someone with the relevant expertise can clearly follow:

how the research was done;
why it was done in that way;
the evidence that was established;
the reasoning and judgements that were used;
how these led justifiably to the research findings and conclusions.




Making the route visible




flowchart LR
    A[Research question] --> B[Data sources]
    B --> C[Data cleaning]
    C --> D[Analysis decisions]
    D --> E[Results]
    E --> F[Findings and conclusions]

    B -. document .-> G[Project record]
    C -. document .-> G
    D -. document .-> G
    E -. document .-> G





Without documentation, others may see only the final conclusion. Reproducible research makes the route to that conclusion visible.

title: Chapter 1 — Why Open Research and Reproducibility?
section: Key terms used in this workshop
text:
Key terms used in this workshop
These terms overlap, but they are not identical.


Open research
Making research outputs, methods and decisions accessible to others, where this is lawful, ethical and appropriate.


Transparency
Clearly documenting what was done, why it was done and how decisions were made, including decisions that may not appear in the final paper.


Reproducibility
Enabling someone else — or your future self — to understand and, where possible, recreate the analytical steps and results using the same data and methods.


A project can be reproducible without being fully open. This is particularly important for research using sensitive, confidential or linked administrative data. Conversely, sharing outputs is not enough for reproducibility if key steps, assumptions, software, metadata or decisions are missing.

title: Chapter 1 — Why Open Research and Reproducibility?
text:
Reproducible is not the only “-ible”
The Turing Way distinguishes between several related concepts4.

Different ways research can be checked or extended




Same analysis
Different analysis




Same data
Reproducible
Robust


Different data
Replicable
Generalisable



These distinctions help clarify what kind of checking is possible. Not every project can support every form of reuse, especially when data are sensitive, access-controlled or context-specific.

The important point is to be clear about what kind of checking, reuse or scrutiny is realistic.

title: Chapter 1 — Why Open Research and Reproducibility?
text:
Principles, practices and tools
This workshop separates principles, practices and tools.


Principles
The underlying aims, such as transparency, accountability, reuse, quality and trust.


Practices
The behaviours that implement those aims, such as documentation, version control, naming conventions and decision logs.


Tools
The software, repositories and platforms that support the practices, such as RStudio, Quarto, Git, GitHub, OSF or institutional repositories.


Tools change quickly. Good practices travel across projects, disciplines and institutions. This is why the workshop focuses on habits and workflows, not only on any single platform.

title: Chapter 1 — Why Open Research and Reproducibility?
Chapter 1 — Why Open Research and Reproducibility?
text:
Where irreproducibility comes from
Irreproducibility is usually not caused by misconduct. More often, it comes from ordinary workflow problems.
Common examples include:

manual data cleaning steps that are not recorded;
unclear file names, folder structures or version histories;
results generated interactively without a saved script;
missing information about software versions, packages or dependencies;
undocumented exclusions, recoding decisions or sensitivity analyses;
project knowledge held by one person and lost when they move on.

These problems often become visible late: during submission, peer review, audit, collaboration or handover. By then, they are harder and more expensive to fix.

Where reproducibility can break down




flowchart LR
    A[Generate research question] --> B[Design study]
    B --> C[Collect or access data]
    C --> D[Clean and analyse data]
    D --> E[Interpret results]
    E --> F[Publish and reuse]

    B -. bias or weak design .-> X[Risk to reproducibility]
    C -. missing provenance .-> X
    D -. undocumented cleaning or p-hacking .-> X
    E -. selective interpretation .-> X
    F -. inaccessible materials .-> X





Some risks are methodological, such as poor design or low statistical power. Others are workflow risks, such as missing documentation, unclear data provenance or inaccessible materials2.







Common risk point



If your workflow depends on remembering what you did, rather than recording it somewhere stable, it is fragile.

title: Chapter 1 — Why Open Research and Reproducibility?
text:
Barriers are real
Working reproducibly takes time, skills and support. Barriers can include lack of incentives, fear of being held to higher standards than others, limited recognition in promotion processes, complex data governance, large computational infrastructure, and uncertainty about what can be shared.
This workshop will not fix all of those structural issues. It focuses on practical improvements that are within the control of individual researchers and teams, while recognising that institutions, funders, journals and senior leaders also have responsibilities.

Quick discussion
Which barrier is most relevant to your own work at the moment?
Is it mainly a personal workflow issue, a team issue, an institutional issue, or a data governance issue?

title: Chapter 1 — Why Open Research and Reproducibility?
text:
Why this helps you too
Open and reproducible practice is often presented as something researchers do for other people: reviewers, funders, journals, collaborators or future users of the work. That is true, but it is also useful for you.



Advantages to sharing your research openly. Source: The Turing Way Community & Scriberia (2021), original version on Zenodo, CC BY 4.0




Avoid disaster
Clear records make it less likely that an analysis becomes impossible to explain, repeat or defend later.


Write papers more easily
Methods, exclusions, decisions and outputs are easier to report when they have been recorded during the project.


Support review and revision
Reviewers, supervisors and collaborators can assess the work more efficiently when the workflow is transparent.


Enable continuity
A well-documented project is easier to hand over when someone moves role, changes institution or returns after a break.


Build reputation
Reliable, well-organised work increases trust in the research and in the team producing it.

title: Chapter 1 — Why Open Research and Reproducibility?
Chapter 1 — Why Open Research and Reproducibility?
text:
What can be made open?
Open research is not a single action. Different parts of the research process can be made more visible, reusable or accessible.


Publications
Open access articles, reports, preprints or plain-language summaries.


Data and metadata
Open data where possible; metadata, documentation and access conditions where data are restricted.


Code and software
Scripts, notebooks, packages, workflows and dependency information.


Methods and materials
Protocols, analysis plans, survey instruments, SOPs and decision logs.


Review and interpretation
Transparent peer review, reporting checklists, sensitivity analyses and clear statements of limitations.

title: Chapter 1 — Why Open Research and Reproducibility?
Chapter 1 — Why Open Research and Reproducibility?
text:
Activity: the six-month test

Individual reflection or small-group discussion
Choose one current or recent project.
Write down one thing that would make it difficult for someone else to understand your results in six months’ time.
Then ask:

Where is this information recorded now?
Would someone else know where to find it?
Would your future self understand it?
What small change would reduce the risk?

title: Chapter 1 — Why Open Research and Reproducibility?
section: Activity: constraints are part of the workflow
text:
Activity: constraints are part of the workflow

Sensitive or restricted data
Identify one constraint that limits what you can share openly. This might be ethical, legal, contractual, governance-related or practical.
Then ask:

Where is that constraint recorded?
Does it explain what can be shared, what cannot be shared and why?
Does it explain how someone could request access, if access is possible?
Is the restriction visible to future project members?

title: Chapter 1 — Why Open Research and Reproducibility?
Chapter 1 — Why Open Research and Reproducibility?
text:
Key takeaways

Open research is now a baseline expectation, not an optional ideal.
Reproducibility problems usually arise from everyday workflow issues, not misconduct.
Sensitive or restricted data can still be handled in transparent and reproducible ways.
Good documentation reduces risk, improves continuity and supports reuse.
The aim is not perfection, but proportionate and defensible practice.

title: Chapter 2 — FAIR basics
text: Chapter 2

title: Chapter 2 — FAIR basics
text:
FAIR basics
FAIR is a quick way to describe good structure. It helps us design research outputs so they can be found, understood, accessed appropriately, and reused responsibly.

title: Chapter 2 — FAIR basics
Chapter 2 — FAIR basics
text:
What FAIR means
FAIR stands for Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable1,2.
In simple terms, FAIR asks whether a research output has been structured and described well enough that someone else — or your future self — can:

discover that it exists;
understand what it is;
know how access is managed;
interpret it correctly;
judge whether and how it can be reused.




FAIR data principles: Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable. (Source: SangyaPundir/Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0.)



FAIR as “good structure”








FAIR element
Practical question
What this means




Findable
Can people discover that this output exists?
Use persistent identifiers, clear titles, keywords and searchable metadata.


Accessible
Is it clear how the output can be accessed?
State access routes, restrictions, permissions and conditions.


Interoperable
Can it work with other systems, tools or datasets?
Use appropriate formats, standards, vocabularies and consistent structure.


Reusable
Can it be used again responsibly?
Provide context, provenance, versioning, licences and limitations.



FAIR is a way to design outputs so they can be found, understood and reused.

The word “output” is important. FAIR is often introduced through data, but the same logic increasingly applies to code, protocols, documentation, workflows, metadata, software, models and other research materials3–5.

title: Chapter 2 — FAIR basics
section: FAIR is not the same as “open”
text:
FAIR is not the same as “open”
A common misunderstanding is that FAIR means “make everything public”. It does not.
An output may be:

FAIR and open, for example an anonymised dataset deposited in a trusted repository with a DOI, metadata and a licence;
FAIR but restricted, for example sensitive health data that cannot be downloaded publicly but has clear metadata, access conditions and governance routes;
not FAIR, even if it is publicly available, for example a spreadsheet uploaded without documentation, variables, licence or version information.







Accessibility does not imply openness



The A in FAIR means that access conditions are clear. It does not mean that everyone must be able to download the data.
For sensitive or restricted-access data, FAIR may mean making the metadata, access process, governance restrictions and reuse conditions clear, while keeping the data themselves protected.


This distinction is particularly important for health, education, administrative and linked data. In these settings, good practice is often not “share the data openly”, but rather: describe the data well, preserve the analysis materials, explain the access route, and document what cannot be shared and why6,7.

title: Chapter 2 — FAIR basics
section: FAIR applies to more than datasets
text:
FAIR applies to more than datasets
The slides emphasise an important point: FAIR applies to more than data. In most research projects, several types of output may need to be structured, described and preserved.


Data
Raw data, processed data, derived variables, analysis-ready datasets, lookup tables and spatial layers.


Code and software
Scripts, functions, packages, notebooks, computational environments and dependencies.


Protocols and plans
Study protocols, analysis plans, preregistrations, data management plans and decision logs.


Documentation
READMEs, metadata, data dictionaries, codebooks, SOPs, variable definitions and file inventories.


Workflows
Steps linking data collection, cleaning, analysis, outputs, archiving and reuse.


You do not have to make everything FAIR at once. Some materials will be more important than others. A good starting point is to ask: what would someone need in order to understand, check or reuse this work later?

title: Chapter 2 — FAIR basics
Chapter 2 — FAIR basics
Chapter 2 — FAIR basics
Chapter 2 — FAIR basics
Chapter 2 — FAIR basics
section: How to make data more FAIR
text:
How to make data more FAIR
The slides summarise six practical actions. These are not the only FAIR actions, but they are a useful starting point.


1. Document your data
Explain the data clearly so others — and your future self — can understand and use them. Include a README, data dictionary, codebook and any code needed to interpret or recreate derived variables.


2. Add metadata
Metadata help others find, understand and reuse the data. Use repository metadata fields and, where relevant, discipline-specific metadata standards8,9.


3. Use appropriate formats
Use open, machine-readable or widely used formats where possible. Avoid formats that require proprietary software unless there is a clear reason10.


4. Make access clear
Share openly where possible. If access must be restricted, explain who can access the output, under what conditions, and through what route.


5. Link and cite outputs
Use persistent identifiers, such as DOIs, where possible. Cite datasets, code and other outputs in publications and reports. Trusted repositories make this easier because they normally provide stable records, metadata and persistent identifiers11.


6. Add a licence or reuse statement
Specify what others are allowed to do with the material. For code, data and documentation, licences may differ, so state the conditions clearly12,13.


These actions link directly to later chapters on project organisation, documentation, code quality, publishing, persistent identifiers and preparing for reuse.

title: Chapter 2 — FAIR basics
section: FAIR and sensitive or restricted data

title: Chapter 2 — FAIR basics
text:
FAIR, data management and DMPs
FAIR principles are closely related to data management, but they are not the same thing.

How FAIR relates to data management




flowchart LR
    A[FAIR principles] --> B[Data management practice]
    B --> C[Data Management Plan]
    C --> D[Project delivery and preservation]

    A --> A1[Design outputs so they can be found, accessed, understood and reused]
    B --> B1[Organise files, document variables, manage versions, record decisions]
    C --> C1[Explain what will be done, why, by whom and with what resources]
    D --> D1[Preserve, publish, archive or restrict outputs appropriately]






FAIR provides the design principles. Data management practice implements those principles through day-to-day organisation, documentation, versioning, storage, security and preservation. The Data Management Plan explains how these practices will be applied, justified and resourced.

title: Chapter 2 — FAIR basics
text:
FAIR and the research lifecycle
FAIR is not only something to think about at publication. It applies across the whole project lifecycle.

FAIR across the research lifecycle




flowchart LR
    A[Plan and design] --> B[Collect or create]
    B --> C[Store and manage]
    C --> D[Analyse and collaborate]
    D --> E[Share and disseminate]
    E --> F[Publish and reuse]
    F --> A

    A -. FAIR questions .-> A1[What outputs will exist?]
    B -. FAIR questions .-> B1[How will they be structured?]
    C -. FAIR questions .-> C1[How will they be described and protected?]
    D -. FAIR questions .-> D1[How will code, versions and decisions be recorded?]
    E -. FAIR questions .-> E1[What can be shared, restricted or cited?]
    F -. FAIR questions .-> F1[How will outputs remain reusable?]






This lifecycle framing is useful because FAIR decisions are harder to fix at the end. For example, it is much easier to produce a useful data dictionary if variable names, coding decisions and derived variables have been documented throughout the analysis.

title: Chapter 2 — FAIR basics
text:
Data integrity: the ALCOA principles
FAIR is about making research outputs findable, accessible, interoperable and reusable. It should be complemented by data integrity principles, which focus on whether data are reliable, traceable and trustworthy.
The ALCOA framework describes core characteristics of reliable records:


Attributable
It should be clear who created, collected, modified or approved the data.


Legible
The record should be readable, understandable and interpretable over time.


Contemporaneous
Data should be recorded at the time the activity occurred, or as soon as possible afterwards.


Original
The original record, or an authorised copy, should be retained.


Accurate
Data should correctly reflect what was observed, measured or derived.


ALCOA is useful in this workshop because it makes data integrity concrete. It asks simple questions: Who did this? When was it done? Can the record be read? Is it the original? Is it correct?

title: Chapter 2 — FAIR basics
text:
ALCOA+ and ALCOA++
Many organisations extend ALCOA to ALCOA+ and ALCOA++. The exact wording varies, but the additional principles usually emphasise completeness, consistency, durability and ongoing review.

From ALCOA to ALCOA+ and ALCOA++



ALCOA
ALCOA+
ALCOA++




Attributable
Complete
Integrity


Legible
Consistent
Robustness


Contemporaneous
Enduring
Transparency


Original
Available
Accountability


Accurate

Reliability



These extensions are useful because reproducibility is not only about whether a file exists. It also depends on whether the record is complete, consistent, protected from inappropriate change, available for review, and accountable.

For routine research practice, this does not mean adding another complicated framework. It means checking whether your project records are traceable, understandable and defensible.



ALCOA-CCEA principles for data integrity: Attributable, Legible, Contemporaneous, Original, Accurate, Complete, Consistent, Enduring and Available. (Source:Kakomat/Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0.)

title: Chapter 2 — FAIR basics
text:
Bringing FAIR and ALCOA together
FAIR and ALCOA answer slightly different questions.

Two complementary questions








Framework
Main question
Practical focus




FAIR
Can the output be found, accessed appropriately, interpreted and reused?
Metadata, identifiers, access routes, formats, documentation, licences.


ALCOA
Can the record be trusted as reliable and traceable?
Attribution, timing, legibility, originality, accuracy, completeness and accountability.



Together, they support research outputs that are both usable and trustworthy.

title: Chapter 2 — FAIR basics
section: Activity: apply FAIR to one output
text:
Activity: apply FAIR to one output

Individual reflection or small-group discussion
Choose one research output from a current or recent project. This could be a dataset, script, protocol, report, data dictionary, map, figure, model or workflow.
For that output, ask:

Findable: Could someone discover that it exists?
Accessible: Is it clear who can access it, and under what conditions?
Interoperable: Could it be opened, interpreted and used correctly in another context?
Reusable: Is there enough context, documentation and permission information for responsible reuse?

Then identify the weakest FAIR element and one practical improvement.

title: Chapter 2 — FAIR basics
text:
Activity: quick ALCOA check

Record integrity check
Choose one important file or decision record from your project.
Ask:

Attributable: Is it clear who created or changed it?
Legible: Would someone else understand it?
Contemporaneous: Was the information recorded at the time, or reconstructed later?
Original: Is it clear which version is the original or authoritative record?
Accurate: Has it been checked against the source or decision it represents?

Use this as a quick diagnostic, not as a formal audit.

title: Chapter 2 — FAIR basics
text:
Recommended resources for this chapter
These resources are useful if you want to go beyond the workshop slides. I would treat them as a small curated reading list rather than as material everyone must read in full.


Core FAIR references

Wilkinson et al. (2016) — the original FAIR Guiding Principles paper1.
GO FAIR: FAIR principles — concise description of the FAIR principles and their sub-principles2.
The Turing Way: FAIR principles — accessible explanation of FAIR in relation to reproducible research and research data management3.
FAIR in biological practice — practical teaching material for applying FAIR ideas in research workflows4.



Practical implementation resources

Research Data Management guidance
https://library-guides.ucl.ac.uk/research-data-management/best-practices-fair-data
Cambridge Research Data Management: FAIR principles — useful plain-language explanation of FAIR14.
DCC disciplinary metadata standards and Cambridge metadata guidance — useful when deciding what metadata standards or fields to use8,9.
Cambridge file formats guidance — useful for explaining why open, machine-readable and widely used formats matter10.
Cambridge repository guidance — useful when deciding where to deposit data or other outputs11.



Sensitive data, access statements and licensing

OpenAIRE sensitive data guide — useful for explaining why sensitive data can still be preserved and described safely6.
Cambridge data access statement guidance — useful for writing publication statements when data are open, restricted or unavailable7.
Cambridge Creative Commons and software/code licensing guidance — useful for explaining why data, documentation and code may need different licences12,13.
Horizon 2020 FAIR data management guidance — useful historical/policy framing for FAIR data management and DMP expectations15.

title: Chapter 2 — FAIR basics
Chapter 2 — FAIR basics
text:
Key takeaways

FAIR is a way to describe good structure for research outputs.
FAIR does not mean “make everything public”.
Accessibility means clear access conditions, not necessarily open access.
FAIR applies to data, code, protocols, documentation and workflows.
FAIR actions include documentation, metadata, appropriate formats, access statements, persistent identifiers and licences.
ALCOA complements FAIR by focusing on data integrity, traceability and trustworthiness.
The aim is proportionate, defensible practice — not perfection.

title: Chapter 3 — Project organisation and continuity
text: Chapter 3

title: Chapter 3 — Project organisation and continuity
text:
Project organisation and continuity
Reproducibility often lives in ordinary project habits: where files are kept, how they are named, how versions are handled, and whether someone joining the project later can understand what is going on.

title: Chapter 3 — Project organisation and continuity
Chapter 3 — Project organisation and continuity
Chapter 3 — Project organisation and continuity
section: A project is more than a folder
text:
A project is more than a folder
A folder is only a storage location. A project is a set of linked materials, decisions, people, constraints and outputs. A reproducible project needs more than somewhere to put files.
At minimum, a project needs five things.


Clear purpose
A short statement of what the project is trying to do, what is in scope, and what is out of scope.


Defined outputs
An agreed view of what the project will produce: manuscript, report, dataset, code, figures, policy brief, teaching material, archived record or internal analysis.


Shared conventions
Agreed rules for folder structure, file names, versioning and where key information is recorded.


Protected provenance
A structure that preserves original data and makes it clear how derived files and outputs were created.


Orientation for others
A README or equivalent starting point that explains how the project is organised and what someone should read or run first.


Formal responsibilities and governance are important, but they are covered in Chapter 4: Data Management Plans. This chapter focuses on the day-to-day implementation: the things people see when they open the project.

title: Chapter 3 — Project organisation and continuity
text:
Start with the project boundary
Before designing a folder structure, clarify the boundary of the project. This is especially important when projects share datasets, code, collaborators or outputs.
Ask:

What is the main research question or purpose?
What are the expected outputs?
What data sources are in scope?
Which files are inputs, intermediate products and final outputs?
Which parts of the workflow are exploratory and which are intended to be repeatable?
Which files need to be preserved, and which can be regenerated?
What should someone not edit?

A project boundary does not need to be perfect. It needs to be explicit enough that people know where the work starts, where it ends, and what belongs elsewhere.

Project organisation as a reproducibility layer




flowchart LR
    A[Project purpose] --> B[Folder structure]
    B --> C[File naming]
    C --> D[Versioning]
    D --> E[README]
    E --> F[Handover and reuse]

    B --> G[Raw data protected]
    B --> H[Derived data traceable]
    B --> I[Outputs reproducible]





A good project structure does not make the research correct on its own. It makes the work easier to inspect, maintain and rerun.

title: Chapter 3 — Project organisation and continuity
section: Folder structure: choose a pattern and document it
text:
Folder structure: choose a pattern and document it
There is no single correct folder structure. The best structure depends on the type of project, the outputs, the team, the data sensitivity, and whether the project is mainly exploratory, analytical, teaching-focused or publication-focused.
The important thing is to choose a pattern and document it. Data Carpentry’s guidance on file naming and project organisation highlights that consistency matters more than inventing a perfect system5. Examples from the Good Enough Project and other reproducible science resources show that simple, stable structures are often sufficient6,7.

Common ways to organise a project
Two common approaches are to organise by file type or by workflow stage.









Pattern
Example folders
Works well when
Main risk




By file type
data/, scripts/, figures/, docs/
Small to medium projects with clear scripts and outputs
Outputs may become disconnected from the analysis that produced them


By workflow stage
01_import/, 02_clean/, 03_analysis/, 04_outputs/
Projects with sequential analytical steps
Can become rigid if the project changes


By output
paper1/, policy_brief/, teaching_materials/
Projects with multiple outputs from shared inputs
Shared data/code may be duplicated or edited inconsistently


Hybrid
stable top-level folders plus numbered scripts
Most research projects
Needs a README so the logic is visible



A hybrid structure usually works best for research projects: stable top-level folders, numbered scripts for sequence, and clear separation between raw data, derived data and outputs.


A baseline folder structure
For many research projects, a simple structure is enough.
project-name/
├── README.md
├── data/
│   ├── raw/
│   ├── processed/
│   └── metadata/
├── scripts/
│   ├── 01_import.R
│   ├── 02_clean.R
│   ├── 03_analysis.R
│   └── 04_outputs.R
├── outputs/
│   ├── tables/
│   ├── figures/
│   └── reports/
├── docs/
│   ├── decision-log.md
│   ├── changelog.md
│   └── notes/
└── references/
This is only a starting point. Adapt it to the project. For example, a qualitative project might need transcripts/, coding_framework/ and analysis_memos/. A spatial project might need gis/, shapefiles/, rasters/ or exposure_surfaces/. A systematic review might need searches/, screening/, extraction/ and quality_appraisal/.



Project-based directory organization (Source: https://ecorepsci.github.io/reproducible-science/project-organization.html)





Activity-based directory organisation. (Source: https://ecorepsci.github.io/reproducible-science/project-organization.html





(Source: https://datamanagement.hms.harvard.edu/plan-design/directory-structure)





(Source: http://nikola.me/folder_structure.html)





(Source: https://github.com/bvreede/good-enough-project)








Keep it boring



A good folder structure should be unsurprising. People should not need specialist knowledge of your memory or habits to find the data, scripts, outputs or documentation.

title: Chapter 3 — Project organisation and continuity
text:
Raw, derived and output files
The most important organisational distinction is between raw data, derived data and outputs.


Raw data
The original data as received, downloaded, extracted or collected. These files should be preserved and not edited manually.


Derived data
Files created from raw data through cleaning, linkage, recoding, filtering, aggregation or analysis.


Outputs
Tables, figures, models, reports, manuscripts or other materials produced from the data and code.



Never overwrite raw data
Raw data should be treated as read-only. If changes are needed, make the changes in code and write the result to a derived or processed folder. This gives you a route back if something goes wrong.
A practical pattern is:
data/raw/          original input files, never edited
data/processed/    cleaned or linked files created by scripts
outputs/           tables, figures, reports and model outputs
scripts/           code that generates processed data and outputs
For sensitive data, the actual storage location may be a secure drive or trusted research environment rather than the project folder. The same logic still applies: raw inputs should be protected, transformations should be traceable, and derived outputs should be clearly labelled.






Common risk point



If the only copy of the cleaned data has been manually edited in Excel, and no one can reconstruct what changed, the analysis is fragile.

title: Chapter 3 — Project organisation and continuity
text:
Project-level README
A README is the front door of a project. It should help a new person understand what the project is, where things are, and what to do first.
A project README does not need to be long. It should answer five basic questions.







Question
What to include




What is this project?
Purpose, scope, key outputs, status


What does it contain?
Main folders and file types


What are the main data sources?
Data origin, restrictions, raw/processed distinction


How does the workflow run?
Main scripts, order, software requirements


What are the rules?
Naming conventions, versioning, licence/access conditions, contact person



A README is not a substitute for a DMP, data dictionary, SOP or decision log. It is the orientation document that points to them.

A minimal README template
# Project title

## Purpose
One paragraph describing the aim, scope and main outputs.

## Project status
Active / paused / archived / teaching example / publication support.

## Folder structure
Brief description of each top-level folder.

## Data sources
List main data sources and where access restrictions are documented.

## How to reproduce key outputs
Explain which scripts to run, in what order, and any software requirements.

## Naming and versioning conventions
State the file naming pattern used in this project.

## Documentation
Link to the decision log, changelog, data dictionary, DMP and any SOPs.

## Contact
Name or role responsible for maintaining the project record.

title: Chapter 3 — Project organisation and continuity
text:
File naming: make meaning visible
File names are a form of documentation. Good file names help people identify the content, status and date of a file without opening it. Poor file names create ambiguity, especially during collaboration and handover.
CESSDA and Data Carpentry both recommend descriptive, consistent and machine-friendly names2,5.

Good file names usually

are descriptive enough to be understood without opening the file;
use consistent elements in a consistent order;
avoid spaces and special characters;
use ISO-style dates (YYYY-MM-DD) where dates are needed;
use leading zeros for numbered files (01, 02, 03);
include a version number where versions need to be visible;
avoid jokes, personal shorthand or names tied to one person.



A simple naming pattern
A useful pattern is:
project_component_description_YYYY-MM-DD_v01.ext
Examples:
eyp_cleaned_addresses_2025-03-15_v02.csv
analysis_modelA_results_2025-03-20_v01.rds
manuscript_methods_draft_2025-03-22_v03.docx
decision-log_project_2025-03-22_v01.md
Avoid:
data_new.xlsx
analysis_final_v2_REAL_final2.xlsx
table1_revised_use_this_one.xlsx
jo_notes_latest.docx






“Final” is not a versioning system



Words like final, real_final, latest and use_this_one usually mean the project has lost a clear versioning convention.




Which would you prefer?
analysis_final_v2_REAL_final2.xlsx
or
analysis_modelA_results_2025-03-20_v03.xlsx
The second file name is not perfect, but it tells you what the file is, when it was created, and which version it is.

title: Chapter 3 — Project organisation and continuity
section: Versioning without making this a Git workshop

title: Chapter 3 — Project organisation and continuity
section: Decisions should not live only in email

title: Chapter 3 — Project organisation and continuity
section: A DMP is the agreement; the project structure is the implementation
text:
A DMP is the agreement; the project structure is the implementation
The Data Management Plan records the project’s formal decisions about data sources, storage, access, documentation, retention, sharing and responsibilities. The project structure is where these decisions become everyday practice.







DMP decision
Project implementation




Raw data must not be altered
data/raw/ protected and treated as read-only


Derived files must be reproducible
cleaning scripts write to data/processed/


Access is restricted
restricted files stored in approved secure location


Outputs must be archived
final outputs placed in a release or archive folder


Data must be documented
data dictionary and README stored in docs/ or data/metadata/


Changes must be traceable
changelog and versioned outputs maintained



This avoids repetition between chapters. Chapter 3 shows what good organisation looks like in practice. Chapter 4 explains how the relevant data-management decisions and responsibilities are recorded.

title: Chapter 3 — Project organisation and continuity
text:
Proportionate project organisation
A small MSc project, a multi-site clinical study, an administrative data linkage project and a national policy evaluation do not need identical structures. The level of organisation should be proportionate to risk, scale and likely reuse.
However, almost every project benefits from a minimum viable structure.


One project root
Keep the project materials in one clear location, or document where restricted materials are stored.


One README
Provide a clear starting point for anyone opening the project.


Protected raw data
Preserve original inputs and avoid manual edits.


Clear naming
Use consistent, descriptive and machine-friendly file names.


Visible versions
Record meaningful changes and avoid ambiguous “final” files.


Documentation links
Make it obvious where the DMP, data dictionary, decision log and code are stored.

title: Chapter 3 — Project organisation and continuity
text:
Activity: project organisation diagnostic

Individual reflection or small-group discussion
Choose one current or recent project.
Assess it using the questions below:

Is there one obvious project root or starting point?
Is there a README or equivalent orientation document?
Are raw, processed and output files clearly separated?
Could someone identify the latest version of the main outputs?
Are file names descriptive and consistent?
Is there somewhere to record decisions and changes?
Are restricted or sensitive materials clearly labelled and protected?

Identify one small change that would make the project easier to understand or hand over.

title: Chapter 3 — Project organisation and continuity
section: Activity: beginner and advanced next steps
text:
Activity: beginner and advanced next steps

Choose one action
Beginner option:
Create or update a project README. Add the purpose, folder structure, data sources and where to find key outputs.
Intermediate option:
Rename one small set of files using a consistent pattern. Do not rename everything at once unless you can update scripts and links safely.
Advanced option:
Create a changelog and record the last three meaningful changes to the project. Include what changed, when, why and who made the decision.

title: Chapter 3 — Project organisation and continuity
text:
What comes next?
This chapter focused on the visible structure of a project: folders, file names, raw-data protection, versioning and the README.
The next chapter explains how these practices connect to the formal Data Management Plan: the document that records data-related decisions, constraints, responsibilities, access conditions and plans for archiving or sharing.

title: Chapter 3 — Project organisation and continuity
text:
Key takeaways

Project organisation is a reproducibility practice, not just an administrative preference.
A project needs a clear purpose, defined outputs, shared conventions, protected provenance and a starting point.
Raw data should be preserved and never overwritten manually.
File names should make meaning, date and version status visible.
Versioning can start simply: dates, version numbers, changelogs and recoverable changes.
The DMP records decisions; the project structure implements them.

title: Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans
text: Chapter 4

title: Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans
section: Data Management Plans as living documents
text:
Data Management Plans as living documents
A Data Management Plan is not just a funder form. It is the written record of what data will be used, how they will be protected, what can be shared, what cannot be shared, and who is responsible.

title: Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans
Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans
Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans
section: A DMP is the agreement; the project structure is the implementation
text:
A DMP is the agreement; the project structure is the implementation
Chapter 3 focused on the practical structure of a project: folders, file names, raw-data protection, versioning and README files. A DMP sits one level above this. It explains the decisions and responsibilities behind those practices.







DMP decision
Where it appears in practice




Data source and permissions
DMP, ethics/governance documents, README


Storage and access controls
Approved secure drive, TRE, institutional storage, access log


Raw-data protection
Read-only raw-data folder or secure source location


Documentation requirements
README, metadata, data dictionary, codebook


Versioning and provenance
Changelog, scripted workflows, archived releases


Sharing and reuse constraints
Data access statement, repository record, licence, access conditions


Retention and archiving
Institutional policy, funder requirements, repository deposit



The DMP does not need to contain every detail of the folder structure. It should state the principles, locations, responsibilities and restrictions clearly enough that the project team can implement them consistently.

title: Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans
Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans
section: A DMP is a living document

title: Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans
section: What a DMP should usually cover

title: Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans
text:
Roles and accountability
Roles are often where good intentions fail. A project may state that data will be documented, archived or shared, but unless someone is responsible for ensuring this happens, the plan may not be implemented.
A simple responsibility matrix can help.










Task
Responsible
Accountable
Consulted
Informed




Data storage and security
Data manager / analyst
PI or project lead
IT / RDM support
Project team


README, metadata and codebook
Researcher / analyst
Project lead
Collaborators
Project team


Code development and review
Analyst / data scientist
Project lead
Methodologists / collaborators
Project team


Ethics and governance compliance
Project lead
PI / institution
Data protection officer / governance team
Project team


Data sharing and archiving
Data steward / researcher
Project lead
Library / repository staff
Project team



You do not need a formal RACI table for every project. The principle is that responsibilities should be visible and realistic. For small projects, a short section in the DMP may be enough. For larger projects, a matrix can prevent confusion.






Responsibility is not an event



Responsibility is not only ethics approval, governance forms or compliance training. It is also everyday decisions: what can be shared, what cannot be shared, why, under what conditions, and who has authority to decide.

title: Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans
text:
Data description, organisation and governance
A common weakness in DMPs is mixing three different questions.


Description
What data exist? What do they contain? Where did they come from? What do variables, codes and files mean?


Organisation
Where are the files? How are they named? How are raw, derived and output files separated? How are versions tracked?


Governance
Who is allowed to access the data? Under what approvals, restrictions, agreements or legal conditions?


A good DMP connects all three. It does not need to include every technical detail, but it should point to where the details are recorded.

title: Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans
section: Responsible sharing does not mean sharing everything
text:
Responsible sharing does not mean sharing everything
Responsible sharing means making clear what can be shared, what cannot be shared, and why. This is especially important for sensitive or restricted data.
A DMP should distinguish between:

data that can be made openly available;
data that can be shared under controlled access;
metadata that can be published even when data cannot;
code that can be shared using synthetic or simulated data;
documentation that can be shared without disclosing confidential information;
outputs that must remain internal because of consent, governance, legal or contractual constraints.







Open does not always mean public



For sensitive or restricted data, good data management may mean discoverable metadata, clear access conditions, preserved documentation, data access statements and secure governance — not public release of the data themselves.



Avoid vague statements
Weak:

Data cannot be shared due to confidentiality.

Stronger:

Individual-level data cannot be made openly available because they contain personal information and are subject to data provider and ethics restrictions. A metadata record, data dictionary, analysis code where possible, and a data access statement will be made available. Requests for access must be directed to [data controller/repository/access committee], subject to approval.

The stronger version is more useful because it explains the restriction and identifies what can be shared.

title: Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans
Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans
section: Planning for derived and intermediate data
text:
Planning for derived and intermediate data
DMPs often describe the original data but say less about derived data. This can be a problem because derived files are often the files used in analysis and publication.
Ask:

Will the project create cleaned datasets?
Will records be linked across sources?
Will exposures, indices, scores or classifications be derived?
Will geospatial buffers, linkage keys, lookup tables or model outputs be generated?
Which derived files need to be preserved?
Which derived files can be regenerated from code?
Which derived files are sensitive even if they no longer contain obvious identifiers?

For reproducibility, the key issue is whether derived data can be traced back to the source data and code that created them.

title: Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans
section: What assessors and reviewers look for

title: Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans
text:
UCL resources and support
For UCL projects, start with UCL’s research data management guidance and the current UCL DMP template3,4. UCL guidance on managing data across the research lifecycle is also relevant for planning, storage, sharing and retention5. UCL also provides guidance on interdisciplinary data management planning10.
DMPonline can help create plans aligned with institutional and funder requirements1.






Use local support early



If the project involves sensitive data, external data providers, linkage, controlled access, NHS or school data, or long-term archiving, involve research data management, information governance, ethics and library support early. It is much easier to design a workable plan at the start than to repair gaps at publication or close-down.

title: Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans
section: Activity: review a DMP as a decision record
text:
Activity: review a DMP as a decision record

Individual reflection or small-group discussion
Choose one current or recent project. Imagine you are reviewing the DMP.
Ask:

Does it clearly describe the data sources?
Does it say where data are stored and who can access them?
Does it explain what documentation will be created?
Does it state what can be shared, what cannot be shared, and why?
Does it identify who is responsible for storage, documentation, analysis, sharing and archiving?
Does it explain when the DMP will be reviewed or updated?

Identify one section that is too vague and rewrite it as a specific decision.

title: Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans
text:
Activity: constraints and access

Make restrictions visible
Identify one constraint that limits what can be shared openly. This might be ethical, legal, contractual, governance-related or practical.
Then write a short access statement using this structure:

What cannot be shared?
Why not?
What can be shared instead?
Who can be contacted, or what process applies, if access is possible?
Where is this information recorded?

title: Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans
text:
What comes next?
This chapter focused on the formal record of data-management decisions: what will be done, why, under what constraints, and who is responsible.
The next chapter moves from project-level planning to the structure of the data files themselves: tidy data, spreadsheet risks, missing values, exclusions and data dictionaries.

title: Chapter 4 — Data Management Plans
text:
Key takeaways

A DMP is a living record of data-management decisions, not just a compliance form.
The DMP records the agreement; the project structure implements it.
Good DMPs are specific, proportionate and realistic.
Roles and responsibilities should be visible, not assumed.
Responsible sharing means explaining what can be shared, what cannot, why, and under what conditions.
Sensitive or restricted data can still be managed in FAIR, transparent and reproducible ways.

title: Chapter 5 — Organising and tidying data
text: Chapter 5

title: Chapter 5 — Organising and tidying data
text:
Organising and tidying data
This chapter moves from the project folder to the data file itself. The aim is to make datasets easier to check, analyse, document and reuse, without pretending that all real-world data arrive clean or simple.

title: Chapter 5 — Organising and tidying data
Chapter 5 — Organising and tidying data
section: If the data are messy, everything is fragile

title: Chapter 5 — Organising and tidying data
text:
What makes a dataset usable?
A usable dataset is not necessarily perfect. It is one that is sufficiently clear, consistent and documented for its intended purpose.
A usable dataset should make the following visible:


Structure
Rows, columns and tables have clear meanings.


Meaning
Variables, units, categories and codes are documented.


Provenance
It is clear where the data came from and how they changed.


Quality
Missing values, exclusions and known limitations are recorded.


White and colleagues describe practical data-reuse principles that are also useful for the original research team: provide metadata, preserve an unprocessed form of the data, use standard formats, use good null values, and make the data easy to import and understand2.

title: Chapter 5 — Organising and tidying data
Chapter 5 — Organising and tidying data
text:
A minimum spreadsheet standard
Where spreadsheets are used, keep them boring and machine-readable.
A minimum standard is:

one table per sheet;
one header row;
one variable per column;
one observation per row;
no merged cells;
no blank rows or columns inside the data table;
no totals, graphs or notes embedded in the data table;
explicit and documented missing-value codes;
dates entered consistently, preferably using ISO format YYYY-MM-DD;
export or archive an analysis version as .csv where appropriate.


From human-readable to analysis-ready
A human-readable spreadsheet might combine address information in one cell:



address




170 Kessels Road Nathan Qld 4111 Australia


1 Parklands Dr, Southport QLD 4215



An analysis-ready table separates the components that may need to be checked, cleaned or linked:












street_number
street_name
street_type
suburb
state
postcode
country




170
Kessels
Road
Nathan
Qld
4111
Australia


1
Parklands
Drive
Southport
Qld
4215
Australia



This does not mean every dataset must be split into the smallest possible units. It means the structure should reflect how the data will be checked, analysed and reused.

title: Chapter 5 — Organising and tidying data
text:
Tidy data: the core idea
Tidy data is a simple but powerful way to structure rectangular datasets. Wickham summarises tidy data as a structure where each variable is a column, each observation is a row, and each type of observational unit forms a table3.
For practical purposes, start with these three rules:


One variable per column
Each column should contain one kind of information, such as date of birth, postcode, exposure value, or outcome status.


One observation per row
Each row should represent one unit of analysis: for example, one participant, one school, one measurement, one visit, or one time period.


One value per cell
Avoid storing multiple pieces of information in one cell when they may need to be analysed separately.


Tidy data are easier to search, filter, join, visualise and analyse. They also reduce the amount of special-purpose code needed to interpret the file.






Tidy does not mean final



A tidy dataset is not automatically correct, complete or analysis-ready. It still needs provenance, definitions, quality checks and documentation.

title: Chapter 5 — Organising and tidying data
text:
Raw, cleaned and analysis-ready data
Chapter 3 introduced the principle of separating raw, derived and output files. In this chapter, the same principle matters because a dataset can change through several states.


Raw data
The data as received, collected or extracted. These should be protected from overwrite.


Cleaned data
Data where formats, labels and obvious inconsistencies have been standardised. Cleaning steps should be scripted or documented.


Analysis-ready data
Data prepared for a specific analysis, with exclusions, derived variables and linkage decisions applied.


The important point is not the exact folder name. The important point is that someone can tell which version is closest to the original source, which version has been cleaned, and which version was used for a specific result.

title: Chapter 5 — Organising and tidying data
text:
Data provenance and lineage
Data provenance describes where data came from, what happened to them, and how outputs were produced. CESSDA frames data-file structure as part of organising and documenting research data so that files remain understandable over time4.
For each important dataset, you should be able to answer:

What is the original source?
Who provided or created it?
When was it received, extracted or generated?
What cleaning steps were applied?
What variables were derived?
What records were excluded?
Which script or workflow created the analysis-ready version?
Which outputs or publications used this version?


A simple data lineage record





flowchart LR
    A[Raw data received] --> B[Cleaning script]
    B --> C[Cleaned dataset]
    C --> D[Derivation / linkage]
    D --> E[Analysis-ready dataset]
    E --> F[Tables, figures, model outputs]

    B -. record decisions .-> G[Decision log]
    D -. record exclusions .-> G
    E -. describe variables .-> H[Data dictionary]





This is the minimum story someone needs in order to understand how the analysis dataset was produced.

title: Chapter 5 — Organising and tidying data
Chapter 5 — Organising and tidying data
text:
Exclusions and filtering decisions
Exclusions should be visible and justifiable. This is particularly important when exclusions affect sample size, bias, precision or the interpretation of inequalities.
A minimum record should include:







Item
Example




Exclusion rule
Exclude records with missing postcode


Rationale
Exposure assignment requires geocoding


Number excluded
184 records, 3.2% of sample


Timing
Applied before exposure linkage


Script or decision record
02_clean_addresses.R, decision log entry 2025-02-12


Sensitivity check
Results compared with missing-postcode group described separately









Avoid hidden exclusions



Do not delete rows manually and leave no trace. Use a scripted filter where possible, and record the rule and rationale in a decision log, README or analysis note.

title: Chapter 5 — Organising and tidying data
text:
Data dictionaries and codebooks
A data dictionary or codebook explains what each variable means. It is one of the most useful low-burden reproducibility tools.
A simple data dictionary should include:

variable name;
short description;
source dataset;
data type;
units, if relevant;
allowed values or categories;
missing-value codes;
derivation rule, if the variable was created;
notes or known limitations.


Minimal data dictionary example

description
source
type
allowed values / units
missing code

Unique survey response identifier
Census 2021

Not personally identifying

Residential postcode at survey date

Modelled exposure

Derived

title: Chapter 5 — Organising and tidying data
text:
Basic checks before analysis
Some checks are simple but high-value. They do not require a formal testing framework.
Before analysis, ask:

Does the dataset have the expected number of rows and columns?
Are required variables present?
Are key variables the expected type?
Are dates in one format?
Are numeric variables within plausible ranges?
Are categorical variables using expected labels?
Are missing values coded consistently?
Are duplicate records expected or accidental?
Has the analytic sample size changed since the previous version?

These checks belong naturally in the code-quality chapter as well. Here, the focus is on what to check in the data. Chapter 7 will cover how to implement these checks in R code.

title: Chapter 5 — Organising and tidying data
text:
Messy data activity

Activity: identify the risks
Look at the example below and identify what would make it difficult to analyse safely.



Date
Age
Where was the article
Blood type
Preferred Gender




23/1/2023
32
United States

Brisbane, Australia

Questions:

Are dates recorded consistently?
Is Age the right variable, or would date of birth plus date of measurement be more stable?
Are locations recorded consistently?
Is 0 a valid blood type, a missing code, or an error?
What does N/A mean here?
Is Mail a spelling mistake or a valid category?
What has Excel or spreadsheet software changed automatically?








Possible fixes




Use a consistent date format, preferably YYYY-MM-DD.
Consider recording date of birth rather than age if age depends on timing.
Split location into structured fields where useful, such as country, region and city.
Define missing-value codes explicitly.
Use controlled categories for gender and blood type where appropriate.
Keep a data dictionary so future users understand all valid codes.

title: Chapter 5 — Organising and tidying data
text:
Minimum viable practice
For most projects, a realistic minimum is:


Protect the raw data
Keep a copy of the data as received. Do not overwrite it.


Use one analysis dataset
Make clear which cleaned or analysis-ready file was used for each analysis.


Create a data dictionary
Document variables, units, categories and missing codes.


Record exclusions
State what was removed, why, when and by whom.

title: Chapter 5 — Organising and tidying data
text:
Beginner and advanced next steps

Choose one action
Beginner

Decide how missing values will be represented.
Use YYYY-MM-DD for dates.
Create a short data dictionary for one dataset.
Save an analysis-ready copy separately from the raw data.

Advanced

Add automated checks for row counts, variable names and plausible ranges.
Create a scripted cleaning pipeline from raw to analysis-ready data.
Add exclusion counts to the analysis report.
Use controlled vocabularies or metadata standards where appropriate.

title: Chapter 5 — Organising and tidying data
text:
What comes next?
The next chapter moves from data-file structure to documentation. We will look at README files, data dictionaries, metadata, decision logs, changelogs, environment notes and protocols.

title: Chapter 5 — Organising and tidying data
text:
Key takeaways

Messy data make analysis fragile, even when the project folder is well organised.
Spreadsheets are useful but risky when formatting, comments or manual edits carry analytical meaning.
Tidy data principles make datasets easier to check, analyse and reuse.
Missing values and exclusions need explicit definitions and records.
A data dictionary is one of the simplest and most valuable reproducibility tools.
Chapter 7 will show how to implement some of these checks in R code.

title: Chapter 6 — Documentation and protocols
text:
Documentation and protocols
Documentation is how research decisions remain visible after the meeting, the analysis session, the handover, or the paper submission. It should help someone understand what was done, why it was done, and how to continue or check the work.

title: Chapter 6 — Documentation and protocols

Chapter 6 — Documentation and protocols
text:
A documentation map
Different documentation artefacts answer different questions.








Documentation artefact
Main question it answers
Main audience




README
What is this project or dataset, and how do I navigate it?
New collaborator, reviewer, future you


Data dictionary / codebook
What does each variable mean?
Analyst, reviewer, secondary user


Metadata
What is this dataset, where did it come from, and how should it be cited/discovered?
Repository, data steward, future user


Decision log
Why was this choice made?
Project team, reviewer, future analyst


Changelog
What changed, when, and by whom?
Project team, future user


Environment notes
What software context is needed to rerun the work?
Analyst, future user


ELN
What happened during active work?
Project team


SOP
How should this repeatable task be done?
Team member, new staff


Protocol / preregistration
What was planned before data collection or analysis?
Reviewer, future reader, research team




When to use each document — quick guide







Situation
Useful documentation




A new person joins the project
README


A variable needs explaining
Data dictionary or codebook


A dataset needs citing, archiving or discovery
Metadata


A choice needs justifying later
Decision log


A file, output or document changes
Changelog


Code needs to be rerun in the same environment
Environment notes


A task must be done consistently
SOP


A study or analysis plan needs to be visible before results
Protocol or preregistration

title: Chapter 6 — Documentation and protocols
text:
Start with a simple README
A README is the first place someone should look when they enter a project folder, repository or dataset. It does not need to be long. It should orientate the reader.
A project README should usually answer:

What is this project? Purpose, scope, key outputs.
What does it contain? Data files, scripts, documents and folder structure.
How does it work? How to run the analysis or navigate the materials.
What decisions matter? Versions, exclusions, constraints and key assumptions.
What are the rules? Licence, sharing conditions, citation and contact details.

For a dataset, a README may also include data collection methods, variable descriptions, missing-value codes, software requirements and restrictions on reuse3,4.






Keep it findable



Put the README at the highest relevant level: project root, data folder or dataset deposit. A hidden README is not doing its job.

title: Chapter 6 — Documentation and protocols
Chapter 6 — Documentation and protocols
Chapter 6 — Documentation and protocols
text:
Decision logs
A decision log records choices that may matter later. It is especially useful when the “right” choice is not obvious, when decisions are made under constraints, or when alternative choices could affect results.
Decision logs are useful for:

key analytical choices;
exclusions and deviations;
parameter changes;
changes to inclusion criteria;
changes to exposure or outcome definitions;
trade-offs caused by data quality, governance, ethics or feasibility;
choices made after discussion with collaborators or PPIE contributors.

Example:










Date
Decision
Rationale
Impact
Who




2025-02-12
Excluded records with missing postcode
Exposure assignment required geocoding
Reduced sample size by 3.2%
JC


2025-02-18
Defined heatwave as ≥3 consecutive days above 95th percentile
Aligned with agreed threshold; sensitivity tested
Affected exposure prevalence estimates
JC + PH


2025-02-25
Used 100 m buffer for greenspace instead of 300 m
300 m caused high spatial overlap; reduced precision
Changed exposure metric; documented in methods
JC



A decision log can be a simple Markdown file, spreadsheet or table in an electronic lab notebook. The format matters less than whether the log is maintained and findable.

title: Chapter 6 — Documentation and protocols
Chapter 6 — Documentation and protocols
text:
Environment notes
Environment notes document the software context required to rerun an analysis. They become particularly important when projects use R, Python, GIS software, command-line tools or specialist external software.
Environment notes should include:

programming language version, for example R 4.3.1 or Python 3.11;
key packages and versions;
operating system, if relevant;
specialist software or external dependencies;
hardware or processing assumptions, if relevant;
how to recreate the environment.

Example:
Software environment
- R version: 4.3.1
- Platform: Windows 11
- Key packages:
  - tidyverse 2.0.0
  - sf 1.0-14
  - lme4 1.1-35
  - targets 1.3.0

Environment management
- Project uses renv
- Lockfile: renv.lock stored in project root

To recreate:
install.packages("renv")
renv::restore()
This chapter only introduces environment notes. Chapter 7 covers code quality, checks and version-aware coding in more detail.

title: Chapter 6 — Documentation and protocols
Chapter 6 — Documentation and protocols
Chapter 6 — Documentation and protocols
section: Expert bias and the curse of knowledge
text:
Expert bias and the curse of knowledge
Experts often omit details because those details feel obvious. This is one reason protocols can be difficult to reproduce. The person writing the protocol already knows the context, equipment, assumptions and tacit steps; the person reading it may not.
A useful test is:

Could a competent person who is new to the project follow this without asking you?

The answer does not need to be “yes” for every piece of documentation. But for SOPs, data dictionaries, README files and analysis instructions, it should be the aim.

title: Chapter 6 — Documentation and protocols
text:
Onboarding and offboarding
Onboarding and offboarding are documentation opportunities. New team members ask useful questions because they do not yet share the team’s assumptions. Leaving team members are the best people to document what only they know.






Onboarding: quick checklist



Onboarding: quick checklist
When joining a new research project, ask where the key information is stored and how the team expects work to be documented, checked and shared.

Planning

Read the project protocol, data management plan or analysis plan.
Identify the main research question, outputs and intended users.
Clarify roles, responsibilities and who reviews decisions, code and outputs.



Data and governance

Check which datasets are being used and where they came from.
Check any approvals, licences, data sharing agreements or access restrictions.
Understand what can be shared openly, shared under restriction, or not shared.



Workflow and documentation

Review the project folder structure and file-naming conventions.
Find the data dictionary, metadata, codebook or README files.
Check how decisions, assumptions, errors and changes are recorded.



Reproducible analysis

Check where scripts are stored and how they should be run.
Record software versions, packages and dependencies.
Make sure outputs can be regenerated from the underlying data and code.



Sharing and handover

Check which outputs will be shared, deposited or archived.
Confirm how data, code, protocols or reports should be cited.
Before leaving the project, make sure files, scripts and notes are understandable to someone else.










Offboarding: quick checklist



Offboarding: quick checklist
Before leaving a project, make sure another researcher can understand, check and continue your work.

Update the README, project notes or handover document.
Check that files are named clearly and stored in the agreed locations.
Remove or clearly label temporary, duplicate or outdated files.
Make sure scripts can be run by someone else.
Record software versions, packages and dependencies.
Document key decisions, assumptions, exclusions and deviations from the plan.
Check that figures, tables and reports can be traced back to the data and code.
Confirm which data, code, metadata or outputs should be archived or shared.
Note any unresolved issues, limitations or next steps.
Transfer ownership of relevant files, repositories or project tasks.



The Turing Way provides useful onboarding and offboarding checklists that can be adapted for research groups and projects18.
