title: Chapter 6 — Documentation and protocols
text:
Key takeaways

Documentation is how decisions remain visible.
Different documentation artefacts serve different purposes.
A README orientates; a codebook explains variables; metadata supports discovery and citation; logs record decisions and changes.
SOPs are useful for repeatable tasks; protocols and preregistrations make planned methods visible.
Proportionate documentation reduces risk, supports handover and strengthens reproducibility.

title: Chapter 7 — Coding for reproducible research
text: Chapter 7

title: Chapter 7 — Coding for reproducible research
text:
Coding for reproducible research
Good research code is not just code that runs. It is code that another person — including your future self — can understand, rerun, check, and adapt.

title: Chapter 7 — Coding for reproducible research
Chapter 7 — Coding for reproducible research
Chapter 7 — Coding for reproducible research
text:
Write code for humans first
Most research code will be read more often than it is written. This means code should be written for human understanding, not only for the computer.
A useful principle is: make the logic visible.
For example, avoid short or ambiguous object names:
x1 <- d %>% f() %>% g() %>% h()
Prefer names that describe the analytical step:
clean_data <- clean_addresses(raw_data)

linked_data <- link_to_uprn(clean_data)

model_data <- derive_exposure(linked_data)
This is not only a style issue. Clear names help someone understand what each object represents, where it came from, and what it should be used for.

Naming and structure
Use:

one consistent naming convention, such as snake_case;
meaningful names for variables, files, scripts, and functions;
scripts separated by purpose;
a stable folder structure;
filenames that sort in the order they should be run.

For example:
01_import_data.R
02_clean_data.R
03_link_addresses.R
04_derive_exposures.R
05_model_outcomes.R
06_create_outputs.R
Avoid:

spaces in filenames;
ambiguous names such as final_final_analysis.R;
mixing different naming styles in the same project;
scripts that do many unrelated things.

The Tidyverse style guide is a useful reference for R coding style, especially for naming, spacing, and readable syntax.

title: Chapter 7 — Coding for reproducible research
section: Start each script with basic orientation
text:
Start each script with basic orientation
Every script should begin with enough information for someone else to know what it is and how it fits into the project.
A simple header might include:
# Title: Create descriptive PM2.5 summary table
# Author: Joana Cruz
# Date: 2026-03-02
# Purpose: Imports cleaned nursery PM2.5 data and creates Table S1.
# Inputs: outputs/dataset_unique.rds
# Outputs: documents/manuscript/tables/table_s1_pm25_summary.html
# Notes: Requires cleaned dataset generated by 02_clean_data.R
This is a small habit with high value. It reduces confusion, supports handover, and makes scripts easier to review.






Practical habit



Before writing new code, add a short header. It takes less than two minutes and can save hours later.

title: Chapter 7 — Coding for reproducible research
section: Comment the why, not only the what
text:
Comment the why, not only the what
Comments should explain reasoning, assumptions, and unusual decisions.
Poor comments often restate the code:
# Calculate mean
mean_pm25 <- mean(pm25)
Better comments explain why the step matters:
# Use annual mean PM2.5 because the exposure model provides one estimate per provider-year.
mean_pm25 <- mean(pm25, na.rm = TRUE)
Useful comments often explain:

why an exclusion was made;
why a threshold was chosen;
why a transformation was needed;
why a sensitivity analysis was added;
why one method was preferred over another.

Avoid commenting every line. Too many comments can make code harder to read, and outdated comments can mislead. The aim is to document the analytical reasoning that would not be obvious from the code alone.

title: Chapter 7 — Coding for reproducible research
Chapter 7 — Coding for reproducible research
Chapter 7 — Coding for reproducible research
text:
Use project-relative paths
Hard-coded paths are one of the most common barriers to rerunning code.
Avoid paths like:
"C:/Users/name/Documents/project/data/file.csv"
These will usually only work on one computer.
Instead, use project-relative paths. In R, the here package is useful:
data_dir <- here::here("data", "processed")
figures_dir <- here::here("documents", "manuscript", "figures")
tables_dir <- here::here("documents", "manuscript", "tables")
If output folders are needed, create them in the script:
fs::dir_create(figures_dir)
fs::dir_create(tables_dir)
This means the project can be moved to another computer without rewriting every path, provided the folder structure stays the same.

title: Chapter 7 — Coding for reproducible research
text:
Check before you analyse
Testing does not have to mean formal software engineering.
In research workflows, simple checks can prevent serious errors. The aim is to fail early, with a clear message, rather than continue silently with invalid data.

Check that files exist
missing_files <- input_paths[!file.exists(input_paths)]

if (length(missing_files) > 0) {
  stop(
    "These required input files are missing:\n",
    paste(missing_files, collapse = "\n")
  )
}


Check that the dataset is not empty
if (nrow(nursery_pm25_data) == 0) {
  stop("The dataset has 0 rows. Analysis cannot continue.")
}


Check that required variables exist
required_vars <- c(
  "year",
  "pm25",
  "pm25_cat",
  "pm25_int",
  "idaci_q",
  "ethnicity",
  "ruc_nm",
  "a_rd_ds_cat"
)

missing_vars <- setdiff(required_vars, names(nursery_pm25_data))

if (length(missing_vars) > 0) {
  stop(
    "The dataset is missing these required variables: ",
    paste(missing_vars, collapse = ", ")
  )
}


Check variable types
Variable-type checks should reflect what the analysis needs.
For example, if pm25 will be summarised numerically:
if (!is.numeric(nursery_pm25_data$pm25)) {
  stop("Variable 'pm25' must be numeric.")
}
For variables such as year, be careful. It may be correct for year to be numeric during validation and then converted to a factor for plotting. Alternatively, if your dataset already stores year as a factor, the check should allow that.
For example:
if (!is.numeric(nursery_pm25_data$year) &&
    !is.integer(nursery_pm25_data$year) &&
    !is.factor(nursery_pm25_data$year)) {
  stop("Variable 'year' should be numeric, integer, or factor.")
}
The key point is not the exact rule. The key point is to make the expectation explicit.

title: Chapter 7 — Coding for reproducible research
text:
Check missingness and exclusions
Missing data do not automatically invalidate an analysis, but they need to be understood and documented.
A simple missingness summary is often a useful first check:
missingness_summary <- tibble::tibble(
  variable = required_vars,
  n_missing = sapply(nursery_pm25_data[required_vars], function(x) sum(is.na(x))),
  pct_missing = round(
    sapply(nursery_pm25_data[required_vars], function(x) mean(is.na(x)) * 100),
    2
  )
)

missingness_summary
For reproducibility, exclusions should not be hidden inside code. They should be:

visible in the script;
justified in comments or a decision log;
reported in outputs where relevant.

For example:
# Exclude records with missing postcode because exposure assignment requires geocoding.
# Decision recorded in docs/decision-log.md, 2025-02-12.
analysis_data <- nursery_pm25_data %>%
  dplyr::filter(!is.na(postcode))
This links coding practice back to documentation and decision capture.

title: Chapter 7 — Coding for reproducible research
section: Build in checks as you go

title: Chapter 7 — Coding for reproducible research
Chapter 7 — Coding for reproducible research
section: Versioning: do not overwrite without trace
text:
Versioning: do not overwrite without trace
Code changes should be recoverable.
At a basic level, this means avoiding uncontrolled overwriting of scripts, outputs, or analysis decisions.
At a stronger level, it means using version control.
Version control allows you to:

track what changed;
see who changed it;
recover earlier versions;
collaborate more safely;
create a transparent analysis history.

Git is the most widely used version control system for code. GitHub is a platform that can host Git repositories and support collaboration.
Version control is not only for advanced programmers. Even for small projects, it can act as an “undo” button for your analysis.






Minimum viable versioning



If Git feels too much at the start, do not let that stop you from improving traceability. Use clear filenames, a short changelog, and a stable folder structure while you build confidence with Git.

title: Chapter 7 — Coding for reproducible research
text:
Traceability without overcomplication
Not every project needs a complex Git workflow. But every project needs some form of traceability.
At minimum, you should be able to answer:

Which script produced this output?
Which data file was used?
Which version of the analysis was run?
What changed since the last version?
Why was that change made?

A simple analysis history might include:
docs/
  decision-log.md
  changelog.md

R/
  01_import_data.R
  02_clean_data.R
  03_create_outputs.R

documents/
  manuscript/
    tables/
    figures/
This links code, documentation, and outputs.
If you are not yet using Git, a changelog is still useful. If you are using Git, a changelog and decision log can still help document reasoning that is not obvious from code changes alone.

title: Chapter 7 — Coding for reproducible research
Chapter 7 — Coding for reproducible research
Chapter 7 — Coding for reproducible research
text:
Sustainable code and green computing
Sustainable code saves time, energy, storage and attention.
This is not only about environmental sustainability, although that matters. It is also about avoiding wasted human effort. In reproducible research, green computing is mostly about avoiding unnecessary reruns, duplicated files, inefficient workflows, excessive resolution, and code that is so unclear that people have to redo work.
Common sources of waste include:

rerunning an entire analysis because intermediate outputs were not saved;
duplicating large files because folder structure and versioning are unclear;
using unnecessarily high spatial or temporal resolution by default;
running models repeatedly because parameters are edited manually;
abandoning and rewriting code because the original script is too difficult to understand;
saving many near-identical output files without clear names or retention rules.

This matters because inefficient workflows cost time, attention, storage and energy. They can also make research less reproducible: if an analysis is too slow, expensive or fragile to rerun, it becomes harder to check, extend or audit.






Green computing is also a reproducibility issue



A workflow that can only be rerun with substantial effort, specialist infrastructure or excessive compute is less accessible to others. Clear, modular and restartable code supports both reproducibility and sustainability.



Low-burden green coding practices
For most projects, the highest-value steps are simple:

Make scripts restartable
Do not require the whole analysis to rerun from the beginning after every small change.
Save useful intermediate outputs
Save cleaned or derived datasets when they are costly to recreate, but document how they were produced.
Rerun only what changed
Avoid manually rerunning everything when only one table, model or figure has changed.
Use appropriate resolution
Choose spatial, temporal or simulation resolution that is justified by the research question, not simply the maximum available.
Check before running expensive steps
Validate inputs before running long analyses or models.
Keep code readable
Readable code reduces unnecessary rewriting, duplicated effort and avoidable reruns.
Delete or archive deliberately
Do not keep unlimited duplicate outputs. Decide what needs to be preserved, archived or removed.



Example from the R Markdown workflow
The example R Markdown file already demonstrates several green computing principles:

it checks that input files exist before analysis starts;
it validates key variables before producing outputs;
it saves tables and figures to predictable folders;
it uses a reusable plotting function rather than repeated code;
it creates outputs automatically when the report is knitted.

The next step would be to make the workflow more restartable, for example by saving cleaned intermediate datasets, recording package versions with renv, or using a workflow tool such as targets for larger projects.

title: Chapter 7 — Coding for reproducible research
Chapter 7 — Coding for reproducible research
section: /Worked example: what the R Markdown file demonstrates
text:
/Worked example: what the R Markdown file demonstrates
The accompanying R Markdown example shows several reproducible coding practices.

1. Project orientation
The report starts by explaining its purpose:

what it does;
what it produces;
why the workflow is structured in this way.

This supports reuse and review.


2. Package management
The report lists required packages and loads them at the start. This makes dependencies visible.
For long-term reproducibility, the next step would be to record package versions using sessionInfo() or manage the environment with renv.


3. Project-relative folders
The report uses here::here() rather than hard-coded paths. This makes the project easier to run on another computer.


4. Input checks
Before importing data, the report checks that all required files exist. This avoids unclear errors later in the workflow.


5. Data validation
The report checks:

whether the dataset has rows;
whether required variables are present;
whether key variables have expected types;
how much missingness is present.

These checks make analytical assumptions explicit.


6. Reusable plotting code
The report defines a plotting function rather than repeating similar ggplot2 code. This supports consistency and reduces copy-paste errors.


7. Predictable outputs
The report saves tables and figures to defined folders. This links code execution to outputs and reduces the risk of manual file handling errors.

title: Chapter 7 — Coding for reproducible research
text:
Practical guidance for early-career researchers
A proportionate starting point is:

Put all code in scripts or Quarto/R Markdown files.
Use project-relative paths.
Give scripts meaningful names.
Start each script with a short explanatory header.
Add simple checks for files, variables, missingness, and impossible values.
Use functions when code is repeated.
Save outputs automatically.
Keep raw data unchanged.
Track changes using Git or, at minimum, a changelog.
Document decisions that are not obvious from the code.

You do not need to do everything at once. Start with one improvement that reduces risk in your current workflow.

title: Chapter 7 — Coding for reproducible research
text:
Exercises

Exercise 1: Review one script
Choose one script from a current or previous project.
Ask:

Is the purpose clear from the top of the file?
Are the inputs and outputs clear?
Are object names meaningful?
Is there repeated code that could become a function?
Are key assumptions checked?



Exercise 2: Add one check
Add one simple check to a script.
For example:

required file exists;
required variable exists;
no impossible values;
expected number of rows;
no duplicated IDs;
missingness below an expected threshold.



Exercise 3: Automate one output
Choose one table or figure that you currently create manually.
Can you regenerate it directly from code and save it to a predictable folder?


Exercise 4: Reduce one source of computational waste
Choose one analysis step that is slow, repetitive or often rerun.
Ask:

Does the whole workflow need to rerun?
Could an intermediate file be saved?
Could the input be checked before running the expensive step?
Is the spatial, temporal or simulation resolution proportionate?
Are duplicate outputs being kept without a reason?

title: Chapter 7 — Coding for reproducible research
text:
Key takeaways

Code quality is part of research quality.
Readable, explicit code is easier to review, rerun, and defend.
Simple checks catch many errors before they affect results.
Versioning makes change safer and more transparent.
Light automation reduces manual work and supports reproducibility.
Sustainable code saves time, energy, storage and attention.

title: Chapter 8 — Publishing, PIDs and preparing for reuse
text: Chapter 8

title: Chapter 8 — Publishing, PIDs and preparing for reuse
section: Publishing, persistent identifiers and preparing for reuse
text:
Publishing, persistent identifiers and preparing for reuse
Publishing is not only about the journal article. It is the point at which research outputs are released, described, cited, licensed and made available for appropriate reuse.

title: Chapter 8 — Publishing, PIDs and preparing for reuse
Chapter 8 — Publishing, PIDs and preparing for reuse
text:
What belongs in this chapter?
Earlier chapters covered how to organise, document and check the work while the project is active. This chapter focuses on what happens when outputs are ready to be shared, archived, cited or made discoverable.


Repositories
Where outputs are deposited, preserved and made discoverable.


Persistent identifiers
How outputs, people and organisations are linked and credited over time.


Access and licensing
Who can access an output, and what they are allowed to do with it.


Reuse package
The documentation, metadata and context that make reuse possible.


Data management plans record decisions; project organisation keeps materials findable; documentation explains methods and decisions; coding makes outputs rerunnable. Publishing brings these together into a stable, citable release.

title: Chapter 8 — Publishing, PIDs and preparing for reuse
Chapter 8 — Publishing, PIDs and preparing for reuse
Chapter 8 — Publishing, PIDs and preparing for reuse
section: Sharing is not the same as enabling reuse

title: Chapter 8 — Publishing, PIDs and preparing for reuse
text:
Repositories: where to deposit outputs
Repositories support preservation, discovery, citation and reuse. They are usually better than personal websites, shared drives or ad hoc file sharing because they provide stable records, metadata and persistent identifiers.
Common repository options include:

institutional repositories, such as the UCL Research Data Repository;
general repositories, such as Zenodo, Figshare or the Open Science Framework;
discipline-specific repositories, where these exist;
software platforms linked to releases, such as GitHub archived through Zenodo;
controlled-access repositories, where data are sensitive or restricted.

Repository choice should be guided by the output type, disciplinary expectations, funder or journal requirements, data sensitivity, file size, access controls and whether the repository issues persistent identifiers.






Sensitive or restricted data



Publishing sensitive data does not usually mean making the data openly downloadable. It may mean publishing metadata, documentation and access conditions, while the data themselves remain controlled, safeguarded or mediated.



Choosing a repository
Useful questions include:

Does the repository accept this type of output?
Does it issue a DOI or other persistent identifier?
Does it support versioning?
Does it allow appropriate access controls?
Does it support clear licensing?
Is it recognised by your funder, journal or discipline?
Will the output be preserved beyond the lifetime of the project?
Can related outputs be linked together?

Repository registries and guidance such as re3data, FAIRsharing, institutional library guidance and The Turing Way can help identify appropriate options.

title: Chapter 8 — Publishing, PIDs and preparing for reuse
Chapter 8 — Publishing, PIDs and preparing for reuse
Chapter 8 — Publishing, PIDs and preparing for reuse
Chapter 8 — Publishing, PIDs and preparing for reuse
Chapter 8 — Publishing, PIDs and preparing for reuse
section: Access and licensing are different decisions
text:
Access and licensing are different decisions
Publishing for reuse usually involves two decisions:

Access route — who can see or obtain the output?
Reuse licence — what can they do with it once they have lawful access?

These decisions are related but not the same.
For example:

data may be openly accessible but licensed for limited reuse;
data may be access-controlled but licensed permissively once access is approved;
metadata may be open even when underlying data are restricted;
code may be open but depend on restricted data.

Separating access from licensing supports clearer governance and more appropriate reuse.






Licence does not override governance



You cannot licence away confidentiality, consent limits, data-provider restrictions or contractual obligations. Access and reuse decisions must remain consistent with ethics, governance and legal constraints.

title: Chapter 8 — Publishing, PIDs and preparing for reuse
text:
Common licence families
Different outputs need different licences.









Output type
Typical licence family
What it enables
Common pitfalls




Papers, figures, reports, READMEs, training materials
Creative Commons licences
Reuse of text, figures and educational material with defined conditions
Restrictive variants can block adaptations, translations or teaching reuse


Metadata records
Often CC0
Maximises discoverability and machine reuse
Metadata must still avoid disclosing confidential or sensitive information


Code and software
Open-source software licences such as MIT, Apache 2.0 or GPL
Reuse, modification and redistribution of code
Licence mismatch across dependencies; unclear ownership in collaborative code


Data
Context-dependent; may be CC0, CC BY or more restricted where lawful
Reuse where sharing is lawful and ethical
Governance, consent and contractual restrictions override licence preferences






Anatomy of a Creative Commons License. Image: Naomitresnalee / Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0.








A proportionate default



For many research projects:

use CC BY for text, training materials and documentation unless there is a clear reason to restrict adaptations;
choose an explicit software licence for code, such as MIT or Apache 2.0, where appropriate;
consider CC0 for metadata where lawful and safe;
for sensitive data, publish metadata and access conditions even when data cannot be open.

title: Chapter 8 — Publishing, PIDs and preparing for reuse

Chapter 8 — Publishing, PIDs and preparing for reuse
section: Context is part of the output

title: Chapter 8 — Publishing, PIDs and preparing for reuse
text:
Preparing a reuse package
A practical reuse package might include:


README
What the output is, what files are included, how they relate, and how to use them.


Metadata
Structured description for discovery, interpretation and citation.


Licence
What reuse is permitted, for each output type.


Access statement
What is available, what is restricted, why, and how access can be requested.


Version information
Release date, version number, changelog and relationship to the paper.


Citation information
How the output should be cited, including DOI and ORCID where relevant.


The level of detail should be proportionate to the output and its likely reuse. A complex linked administrative dataset requires more governance and metadata than a training handout, but both benefit from clear citation, licence and version information.

title: Chapter 8 — Publishing, PIDs and preparing for reuse
Chapter 8 — Publishing, PIDs and preparing for reuse
text:
Practical guidance for researchers
For most projects, a proportionate publishing workflow is:

List the project outputs: paper, data, code, documentation, figures, protocols, notebooks.
Decide which outputs can be open, restricted, described only, or not shared.
Choose suitable repositories.
Prepare README files, metadata and availability statements.
Choose an appropriate licence for each output type.
Create a stable version or release.
Mint or record persistent identifiers.
Link outputs together in the paper and repository records.
Add citation guidance.
Keep a record of what was released and when.

You do not need to publish everything. You do need to make clear, defensible and documented decisions.

title: Chapter 8 — Publishing, PIDs and preparing for reuse
text:
Key takeaways

Publishing is a process of releasing research outputs, not a single event.
Repositories and persistent identifiers support preservation, citation and traceability.
Sharing is not the same as enabling reuse.
Access and licensing are different decisions.
Sensitive data can often be made FAIR through metadata, documentation and controlled access routes.
Preprints, reporting guidelines, code citation and availability statements are part of responsible publishing practice.

title: Chapter 9 — Wrap-up and next steps
text: Chapter 9

title: Chapter 9 — Wrap-up and next steps
text:
Wrap-up and next steps
Open and reproducible research is not a single tool, a single repository, or a final task before submission. It is a set of practical habits that make research easier to understand, check, maintain and reuse.

title: Chapter 9 — Wrap-up and next steps
Chapter 9 — Wrap-up and next steps
section: What this workshop set out to do

title: Chapter 9 — Wrap-up and next steps
text:
The story of the workshop
The workshop followed a workflow logic.


1. Why it matters
We started with open research and reproducibility: why they matter, what they mean, and why they are not the same as making everything public.


2. Good structure
We used FAIR principles and data integrity concepts to think about how outputs can be findable, accessible, interoperable and reusable.


3. Project organisation
We considered how folder structures, file naming, project-level READMEs and lightweight versioning make research easier to navigate.


4. Data management planning
We treated the DMP as a living record of decisions about data, roles, storage, protection, sharing and restrictions.


5. Tidy and analysable data
We looked at the structure of the data file itself: tidy data, spreadsheet risks, missingness, exclusions and data dictionaries.


6. Documentation and protocols
We focused on keeping decisions visible through READMEs, metadata, decision logs, changelogs, SOPs, protocols and reporting guidance.


7. Coding for reproducibility
We brought together readable code, checks, testing, versioning, light automation and sustainable computing.


8. Publishing and reuse
We considered repositories, persistent identifiers, preprints, licences, access statements and preparing outputs for responsible reuse.


The common thread is simple: make the route visible.

title: Chapter 9 — Wrap-up and next steps
text:
Minimum viable reproducibility
Not every project needs the same level of infrastructure. A small student project, a linked administrative data study, a multi-site collaboration and a policy-facing evaluation will have different levels of risk, sensitivity and complexity.
However, most projects benefit from a small set of baseline practices.






Minimum viable reproducibility



If you do only a few things, make them these:

Project README at the root
Explain the project purpose, structure, conventions, contacts and key restrictions.
Raw and derived data separated
Never overwrite raw data. Document how derived datasets were created.
Decisions recorded
Keep a decision log, ELN entry or equivalent record of key assumptions, exclusions, changes and deviations.
Re-runnable analysis
Use scripts or Quarto/R Markdown files rather than manual steps where possible.
Basic checks included
Check files, variables, missingness, IDs and implausible values before analysis.
Planned sharing and access
Record what can be shared, what cannot, why, and under what conditions.
Outputs prepared for citation and reuse
Use ORCID, DOIs where appropriate, licences, access statements and clear metadata.



These practices are deliberately modest. They are not the end point, but they are a strong baseline for transparent and defensible research.

title: Chapter 9 — Wrap-up and next steps
Chapter 9 — Wrap-up and next steps
text:
Practices, not tools
Throughout the workshop, the emphasis has been on practices rather than specific tools.
Tools change. Research contexts differ. What matters is whether the tool supports a clear practice.







Practice
Possible tools




Project organisation
RStudio projects, folder templates, shared drives, GitHub repositories


Documentation
README files, Markdown, Word documents, ELNs, SOPs


Decision capture
Decision logs, lab notebooks, issue trackers, changelogs


Coding and checking
R, Python, Quarto, R Markdown, Jupyter, validation scripts


Versioning
Git, GitHub, changelogs, dated releases


Publishing and citation
Institutional repositories, Zenodo, OSF, DataCite DOIs, ORCID



The tool is useful only if it makes the practice easier to sustain.

title: Chapter 9 — Wrap-up and next steps
text:
Reproducibility as risk reduction
Many open research practices function as risk management.







Risk
Reproducible practice that reduces it




No one knows which file is current
Folder structure, file naming, versioning


A key team member leaves
README, onboarding notes, decision logs


Analysis cannot be rerun
Scripted workflows, project-relative paths, environment notes


Silent data errors affect results
Validation checks, missingness summaries, tests


Data cannot be shared openly
Metadata, access statements, controlled-access routes


Others misuse or misinterpret outputs
Documentation, licences, context and limitations


Code is abandoned or rewritten
Readable code, functions, Git, clear dependencies


Outputs cannot be credited
DOIs, ORCID, citation guidance



Seen this way, reproducibility supports research quality, efficiency and researcher wellbeing. It reduces the mental load of remembering everything and reduces the risk of having to reconstruct decisions under pressure.

title: Chapter 9 — Wrap-up and next steps
Chapter 9 — Wrap-up and next steps
text:
A personal reproducibility plan
Use the prompts below to create a short plan.

Personal action plan
1. One thing I will keep doing
What already works well in your current workflow?
2. One thing I will stop doing
What creates confusion, duplication or avoidable risk?
3. One thing I will start doing
What small habit would make the biggest difference?
4. One person I need to involve
Who needs to agree, know about, or support this change?
5. One place I will record it
Where will this decision or action be documented?

This can be used individually, in pairs, or as a final workshop discussion.

title: Chapter 9 — Wrap-up and next steps
text:
Key takeaways

Open and reproducible research is incremental and contextual.
“Good enough” practice depends on risk, scale and constraints.
Practices matter more than specific tools.
Reproducibility reduces risk, improves continuity and supports responsible reuse.
Small, consistent improvements often deliver the greatest benefit.
Resources, teams and communities can support continued learning.

title: Chapter 9 — Wrap-up and next steps
text:
Thank you
Thank you for taking part in the Open Research and Reproducibility workshop.
The next step is not to make every project perfect. The next step is to make one part of your workflow clearer, safer and easier to understand.
Start small. Make it visible. Build from there.

# One of the most common reproducibility problems is the use of absolute paths,
# for example:
# "C:/Users/name/Documents/project/data/file.csv"
#
# These paths usually only work on one computer.
#
# The 'here' package solves this by building paths relative to the project folder.
# This means the project can be moved to another computer without rewriting paths,
# as long as the folder structure remains the same.

# In this teaching example, the input datasets are stored in outputs because they
# were generated earlier in the workshop. In a full project, these might be stored
# under data/processed or data/derived.

data_dir <- here::here("outputs")
figures_dir <- here::here("documents", "manuscript", "figures")
tables_dir <- here::here("documents", "manuscript", "tables")

# Create output folders if they do not already exist.
# This avoids errors later when saving figures or tables.
fs::dir_create(figures_dir)
fs::dir_create(tables_dir)

# It is often useful to prepare a specific plotting dataset.

plot_data <- nursery_pm25_data %>%

    year = factor(year, levels = 2018:2022),
    idaci_q = factor(idaci_q),

# Rather than repeating almost identical ggplot code several times,
# we define one function that can be reused for different grouping variables.
#
# This improves:
# - readability
# - consistency across plots
# - maintainability
# - sustainability, because it avoids duplicated code and repeated manual edits.

make_pm25_boxplot <- function(data, x_var, x_label, panel_label, x_labels = NULL) {
  
  plot_object <- ggplot2::ggplot(
    data = data,
    ggplot2::aes(
      x = .data[[x_var]],
      y = pm25,
      fill = factor(year)
    )
  ) +
    ggplot2::geom_boxplot(
      outlier.color = "darkgrey",
      color = "lightgrey",
      size = 0.2
    ) +
    ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(
      values = RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(5, "RdYlGn")
    ) +
    ggplot2::geom_hline(
      yintercept = 10,
      linetype = "dotted",
      color = "darkgrey"
    ) +
    ggplot2::labs(
      title = panel_label,
      x = x_label,
      y = "Annual mean concentration of PM2.5 (µg/m³)"
    ) +
    ggplot2::theme_minimal() +
    ggplot2::theme(
      plot.title = ggplot2::element_text(hjust = -0.1, size = 8),
      axis.text = ggplot2::element_text(size = 8),
      axis.title = ggplot2::element_text(size = 8),
      strip.text = ggplot2::element_text(size = 8),
      axis.text.x = ggplot2::element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
      legend.position = "none"
    ) +
    ggplot2::facet_wrap(~ year, nrow = 1, strip.position = "top")
  
  # Optional relabelling of x-axis categories.
  # This is useful when the coded values in the data are less readable
  # than the labels you want to show in the final figure.
  if (!is.null(x_labels)) {
    plot_object <- plot_object + ggplot2::scale_x_discrete(labels = x_labels)
  }
  
  return(plot_object)
}

# 1. Purpose of this report

This report provides a reproducible example of how to:

-   load and manage packages;
-   use project-relative directories rather than hard-coded file paths;
-   import data safely;
-   carry out basic validation checks before analysis;
-   produce a descriptive summary table;
-   create consistent figures using a reusable function;
-   save outputs in a clear and reproducible way;
-   use sustainable coding habits to avoid unnecessary reruns and manual rework.

The aim is not only to produce results, but also to make the workflow understandable, reusable, and easier for someone else to run.

::: callout-note
## Why this example matters

This example brings together several practices from Chapter 7:

-   clear script structure;
-   visible dependencies;
-   project-relative paths;
-   input checks;
-   data validation;
-   reusable code;
-   saved outputs;
-   light automation through R Markdown;
-   sustainable coding through reduced manual repetition.
:::

# 2. Setup





# 3. Load packages


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# In reproducible work, it is helpful to state clearly which packages are needed.
# This avoids situations where code only works on one person's computer.
#
# This helper function is useful for teaching because it makes the script easier
# to run on a new machine. For a real research project, a stronger approach is
# to use renv to record exact package versions.

load_or_install <- function(packages) {
  missing_packages <- packages[!packages %in% installed.packages()[, "Package"]]
  
  if (length(missing_packages) > 0) {
    install.packages(missing_packages)
  }
  
  invisible(lapply(packages, library, character.only = TRUE))
}

# These are the packages used in this report.
#
# here         -> creates project-relative file paths
# fs           -> works with folders and files, including creating directories
# readr        -> useful for reading data files; included here as part of tidy workflows
# dplyr        -> data manipulation
# tibble       -> tidy table objects
# ggplot2      -> plotting
# gtsummary    -> descriptive summary tables
# gt           -> formatting and saving tables
# patchwork    -> combining multiple ggplots into one figure
# RColorBrewer -> colour palettes for plots

5.2 Check that files exist

# Before reading files, check that they exist.
# This is a simple but important validation step.
# It is better to fail early with a clear message than to continue and produce
# a confusing error later.

missing_files <- input_paths[!file.exists(input_paths)]

if (length(missing_files) > 0) {
  stop(
    "These required input files are missing:\n",
    paste(missing_files, collapse = "\n")
  )
}

6.2 Check that required variables exist

# Define the variables needed for this report.
# Writing them out explicitly makes assumptions visible.

required_vars <- c(
  "year",
  "pm25",
  "pm25_cat",
  "pm25_int",
  "idaci_q",
  "ethnicity",
  "ruc_nm",
  "a_rd_ds_cat"
)

missing_vars <- setdiff(required_vars, names(nursery_pm25_data))

  stop(
    "The dataset is missing these required variables: ",
    paste(missing_vars, collapse = ", ")
  )
}

title: ``` R
text:
6.3 Check variable types

# Some variables need to be numeric because we summarise or plot them numerically.
# If a variable has unexpectedly become character, that may indicate a data import
# or recoding problem.

if (!is.numeric(nursery_pm25_data$pm25)) {
  stop("Variable 'pm25' must be numeric.")
}

# For this example, year can be numeric, integer, or factor.
# It may be numeric in the raw/processed data and converted to factor for plotting.

if (!is.numeric(nursery_pm25_data$year) &&
    !is.integer(nursery_pm25_data$year) &&
    !is.factor(nursery_pm25_data$year)) {
  stop("Variable 'year' should be numeric, integer, or factor.")
}

# Show the classes of key variables.
sapply(nursery_pm25_data[required_vars], class)

title: ``` R
text:
6.4 Check missingness

# Missing data do not automatically invalidate an analysis,
# but missingness should be understood and documented.

missingness_summary <- tibble::tibble(
  variable = required_vars,
  n_missing = sapply(nursery_pm25_data[required_vars], function(x) sum(is.na(x))),
  pct_missing = round(
    sapply(nursery_pm25_data[required_vars], function(x) mean(is.na(x)) * 100),
    2
  )
)
